# Transcrição: 05-Editing for Consumption, with Russ Henneberry

- **Fonte:** 05-Editing for Consumption, with Russ Henneberry
- **Duração:** 11:56
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

All right, hey everybody Russ here and I wanted to talk to you about something that I see rarely talked about when it comes to creating blog content particularly, but really any content, any copywriting today on online. One of the considerations that we need to be having with our content is consumption. So I call this consumption and what I want you to realize is that when somebody starts to engage with your copy, they are very similar to a stationary object up at the top of a slide. They need to get to the bottom of the slide and you need to get them in motion to do that. And the way to do that is by editing for consumption or writing for consumption initially, but what I like to do as I go along here, you'll learn the different aspects of consumption, but what I like to do is do an edit with my copy at the end for consumption that allows the reader to get through the copy because if they can't read it or if it looks intimidating and they don't read it or they won't read it, it doesn't do its job. So whatever we're trying to communicate, whether it's an offer or whether it's an idea, it doesn't matter what it is. We need them to consume the copy in order for them to do what we want them to do, which is maybe understand something that we're trying to teach them or buy something or opt into something. So when you're creating copy, we want to do an edit for consumption and this starts with your headline. So what I want you to do is take a minute and access the bonus that you have called the Clicks headline formulas. The click is my email newsletter about marketing and we have something called the Clicks headline formulas and there are just a massive amount of headlines in there. I'm going to show you really quickly this document that you now have access to and just kind of walk you through real quick. There's some notes up here you can read, but the way I like to work with a swipe file like this, a headline swipe file or any kind of swipe file is to sit down and go through it. You know, grab a cup of coffee and read through the headline formulas that are in here. They are organized for you quite nicely. You can click and sort of dive deeper into different topics here like speed and ease. So let's look at some of these speed and ease headline formulas like one simple way to achieve a desired outcome and then we have an example of that headline here. So in your blog post, your headline is the beginning of that slide.

## [03:05 - 06:07]

You want them to A, click on your piece of content whether they saw it on Instagram or Facebook or Twitter or wherever they're seeing it. LinkedIn, they're going to see a headline a lot of times and that needs to pull them in and it needs to start sliding them down the page when they get to your page. So one foolproof way to get your taxes done. So it communicates speed and that's why this headline pulls you in and starts pulling you down the page to read the copy. So what I like to do with a headline, a bunch of headline formulas like this, if I want to get the most out of it is read each one and simply ask the question, why does this work? Why does this formula work? So these formulas are literally everywhere. You will see these on the front of magazines while you're standing in line at the grocery store. It's like get abs in 10 minutes. That's essentially that formula. It's get what you want in a short amount of time or get what you want with ease or speed. So there are a lot of formulas in here. There's ones that communicate novelty like breaking news or this new source. So why does novelty work? Novelty works because people are like, I don't want to read that. I've already read a million things about that. And if you communicate that this is novel or new, then it works. It's like, hey, no, no, you have not seen this before. So this new source is revealing new information or there's a new way to relieve your back pain. So I've been trying to solve my back pain forever and I've read everything. I've tried everything. And so I'm not interested. But when I tell you that there's a new way, that novelty will pull you in. So urgency, threat, and warning. We know why this stuff works. It's because we're wired to grab things when they become scarce. So what I want you to do is take some time and go through these headline formulas and then use the headline formulas when you are writing blog posts. And really, these headline formulas can be used as email subject lines. They can be used as presentation titles, webinar titles, headlines at the tops of sales letters or product pages or whatever you want. They are just persuasive headlines. So step one in consumption is to consider the headline and spend time on it. Use a formula. If you're not great at writing headlines, then use headline formulas. And once you use them for a while, you will start to get so good at writing headlines that you probably won't have to go back into a swipe file like this.

## [06:07 - 09:08]

But to get started, definitely use these. And really, more importantly, read through them all and start to synthesize why they work and that will help you start to write better subject lines, better everything. Now the second part of consumption is the body copy itself. So take a piece of copy like this. This is a blog post from one of my favorite blogs, which is called backlinko.com. And Brian Dean at Backlinko, excellent content marketer and blogger. And so I grabbed this little piece of copy here to illustrate something about consumption. This copy that you're looking at on the page does not look intimidating. It looks approachable. And some of that is about the design and the font and the line height and all that stuff. And that stuff doesn't matter, right? If you go and look at the best blogs online, you're going to see very good user experience when it comes to design and layout. So that's part of it. But the other part of it is the way the copy is written in the first place. So I want you to... So I like to do a test when I look at a piece of copy. Like I will stand back from the copy or just kind of let my eyes kind of go dizzy. I'm not trying to read the page. I am just trying to read the contours of the page and see if it looks approachable. And to do that, what I do is I spin the copy so that it looks like this. Because what I want you to understand is that, again, you have a reader who is stationary at the top of a slide. And think about this as you're riding your bicycle and you see these mountains coming. And what you want your copy to do when you turn it to its side like this is you want it to give them breaks at times. You don't want the copy to look like a mountain that just keeps on going up and it just looks like it's not approachable and you're going to stop that bike and you're not going to try to summit that mountain. You want to give them short sentences. Like... And especially occasionally, you can see as you go along here that this has got peaks and valleys, longer sentences and shorter ones. He probably goes a little overboard, but honestly, this is how I write as well. He's got one sentence per line for the most part. But some rules of thumb here that when you want to improve consumption and you want to create these valleys and you want to create consumable content outside of the design and layout, the way you write, use formatting. Bullets, lists, line breaks, lots of line breaks, rarely more than three sentences in a paragraph.

## [09:08 - 11:56]

Block quotes, transitions. And then of course, one thing that can stop a bike really fast is spelling errors. And honestly, it doesn't do that for me. I don't mind spelling errors. However, even somebody like me, I don't want to see a ton of spelling errors and if I see one or two, it's fine. But some people will not tolerate and will literally bounce right out of your page with spelling mistakes. So you want to edit for consumption. Let's take a look at one of Brian Dean's posts. This is the one that I've swiped this copy from. And let's just look at it from a consumption standpoint. Not the quality of the copy or anything. Just from a is it approachable standpoint? So let's flip this copy, turn it like this and see if we see a scary looking mountain. You can see even the opening here is very short, punchy sentences. And this is designed to start sliding you down the page. You want them to kind of get in motion. You want them to start going down that slide. And you do that by creating copy that is consumable. Short, punchy, tight sentences that don't look scary. There's another good paragraph here, I think. You've got a sentence here and then you've got a numbered list. I'm also a huge fan wherever it makes sense of using images to break up copy and to further illustrate what you are trying to get across in that post. Using images and backlinko spares no expense when it comes to making sure their images are very readable and well designed. So look at this copy and you can kind of get an idea of what I mean when I say approachable and when I say consumable. Because remember that you are not going to get what you want out of that piece of copy if it's not consumable. They will bounce. So using bullets, numbered lists, line breaks, subheadings, all of this stuff matters to us when we're reading. So if you want your copy to be as effective as possible, edit for consumption.
